docs.title: podfive-rs
docs.text: A Rust library and CLI for reading and writing Oxford Nanopore POD5 files.

docs.title: What is POD5?
docs.text:
POD5 is the native file format for Oxford Nanopore sequencing data. It stores:

  • Raw signal data - The electrical current measurements from the nanopore
  • Read metadata - Information about each read (channel, timing, calibration)
  • Run information - Experimental metadata (flow cell, protocol, sample)

docs.title: Why podfive-rs?
docs.text:
podfive-rs provides a fast, memory-efficient Rust implementation for working with POD5 files:

  • Performance - Memory-mapped I/O and efficient compression handling
  • Safety - Rust's type system prevents common errors
  • Simplicity - Clean API for both library and CLI usage
  • Compatibility - Reads and writes files compatible with ONT tools

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
# View reads in a POD5 file
podfive view experiment.pod5

# Merge multiple files
podfive merge -o combined.pod5 run1.pod5 run2.pod5

# Filter by read IDs
podfive filter -i interesting_reads.txt -o subset.pod5 experiment.pod5

docs.title: Library
docs.text:
use podfive_core::Reader;

let reader = Reader::open("experiment.pod5")?;

for read in reader.reads()? {
    let read = read?;
    println!("{}: {} samples", read.read_id, read.num_samples);

    // Get the raw signal
    let signal = reader.get_signal(&read.signal_rows)?;
}

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
• Installation - How to install podfive-rs
  • Quick Start - Get up and running quickly

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• CLI Reference - Command-line tool documentation
  • Library Guide - Using podfive-core in your Rust projects
  • File Format - Technical details of the POD5 format

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: Full API documentation is generated from source code using cargo doc.

docs.title: Generating Documentation
docs.text:
cd podfive-rs
cargo doc --open --no-deps

This opens the documentation in your browser.

docs.title: podfive-core
docs.text: The core library providing POD5 read/write functionality.

docs.title: Main Types
docs.text: Type Description Reader Read POD5 files Writer Create POD5 files WriterOptions Writer configuration ReadData Single read record RunInfoData Run metadata EndReason Read end reason enum Error Error type

docs.title: Modules
docs.text: Module Description compression VBZ compression codec footer FlatBuffer footer parsing reader File reading implementation writer File writing implementation schema Arrow schema definitions types Core data types

docs.title: podfive-cli
docs.text: The command-line interface binary.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text: Command Description view Display reads as table inspect Inspect file contents merge Combine multiple files filter Extract reads by ID

docs.title: Opening Files
docs.text:
use podfive_core::Reader;

let reader = Reader::open("file.pod5")?;

docs.title: Creating Files
docs.text:
use podfive_core::{Writer, WriterOptions};

let writer = Writer::create("output.pod5", WriterOptions::default())?;

docs.title: Read Iteration
docs.text:
for read in reader.reads() {
    println!("{}: {} samples", read.read_id, read.num_samples);
}

docs.title: Signal Access
docs.text: let signal: Vec<i16> = reader.get_signal(&read)?;

docs.title: Run Info
docs.text:
let run_info = reader.get_run_info(read.run_info_index)?;
println!("Sample rate: {} Hz", run_info.sample_rate);

docs.title: Writing Reads
docs.text:
writer.add_run_info(run_info)?;
writer.add_read(read_data, &signal)?;
writer.finish()?;

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
use podfive_core::Error;

match result {
    Ok(value) => { /* success */ }
    Err(Error::Io(e)) => eprintln!("I/O: {}", e),
    Err(Error::InvalidSignature) => eprintln!("Invalid file"),
    Err(e) => eprintln!("Error: {}", e),
}

docs.title: Feature Flags
docs.text: Currently no optional features. All functionality is included by default.

docs.title: podfive-core
docs.text: Crate Purpose arrow Arrow IPC format flatbuffers Footer serialization zstd ZSTD compression uuid UUID handling memmap2 Memory-mapped files thiserror Error derive

docs.title: podfive-cli
docs.text: Crate Purpose clap Argument parsing rayon Parallel processing

docs.title: Minimum Supported Rust Version
docs.text: Rust 1.75 or later is required.

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: The podfive command-line tool provides utilities for working with POD5 files.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: podfive <COMMAND> [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Commands
docs.text: Command Description summary Comprehensive file summary with QC metrics view Display reads as a table inspect Inspect file metadata and contents merge Combine multiple POD5 files filter Extract reads by ID list bam-filter Filter reads based on paired BAM file repack Repack POD5 files to optimize storage subset Split reads into multiple files based on CSV mapping

docs.title: Global Options
docs.text:
-h, --help     Print help information
-V, --version  Print version information

docs.title: Basic Workflow
docs.text:
# 1. Inspect what's in your files
podfive inspect summary run1.pod5
podfive inspect summary run2.pod5

# 2. View the reads
podfive view run1.pod5

# 3. Merge files from a run
podfive merge -o combined.pod5 run1.pod5 run2.pod5

# 4. Extract interesting reads
podfive filter -i selected_reads.txt -o subset.pod5 combined.pod5

docs.title: Working with Multiple Files
docs.text:
Process all POD5 files in a directory:

# List all files
ls *.pod5

# Merge all files
podfive merge -o all_data.pod5 *.pod5

docs.title: Extracting Read IDs
docs.text:
To get a list of read IDs from a file:

podfive inspect reads experiment.pod5 > read_ids.txt

Then filter another file:

podfive filter -i read_ids.txt -o filtered.pod5 other_experiment.pod5

docs.title: podfive bam-filter
docs.text: Filter reads from a POD5 file based on a paired BAM file. Supports filtering by mapped status, genomic region, and mapping quality.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: podfive bam-filter -b <BAM> -o <OUTPUT> [OPTIONS] <INPUT>

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description <INPUT> Input POD5 file or directory

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description -b, --bam <FILE> Input BAM file (required) -o, --output <FILE> Output POD5 file (required) --mapped Keep only mapped reads --region <REGION> Filter by genomic region (chr or chr:start-end) -q, --quality <MAPQ> Minimum mapping quality -h, --help Print help

docs.title: Extract Mapped Reads
docs.text:
Keep only reads that mapped to the reference:

podfive bam-filter -b aligned.bam -o mapped.pod5 --mapped experiment.pod5

docs.title: Filter by Region
docs.text:
Extract reads mapping to a specific chromosome or region:

# All reads on chromosome 1
podfive bam-filter -b aligned.bam -o chr1.pod5 --region chr1 experiment.pod5

# Reads in a specific region
podfive bam-filter -b aligned.bam -o region.pod5 --region chr1:1000000-2000000 experiment.pod5

docs.title: Filter by Mapping Quality
docs.text:
Keep only high-quality alignments:

podfive bam-filter -b aligned.bam -o high_quality.pod5 -q 30 experiment.pod5

docs.title: Combined Filters
docs.text:
Combine multiple filter criteria:

podfive bam-filter -b aligned.bam -o filtered.pod5 \
    --mapped --region chr1 -q 20 experiment.pod5

docs.title: Process a Directory
docs.text:
Filter all POD5 files in a directory:

podfive bam-filter -b aligned.bam -o filtered.pod5 pod5_directory/

docs.title: BAM Index
docs.text:
Region queries require a BAM index (.bai file). If the index doesn't exist, podfive will automatically create one:

Info: BAI index not found, creating aligned.bam.bai...
Done: Created BAI index: aligned.bam.bai

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
The command prints filtering statistics:

Filtering experiment.pod5 using BAM aligned.bam
Output: filtered.pod5
  Filter: mapped reads only
  Filter: region chr1
  Filter: MAPQ >= 20
Scanning BAM file...
50000 read IDs from 75000 BAM records
Scanning POD5 files for dictionary values...
45000 reads found
Filtering [████████████████████████████████████████] 45000/45000
Filtered 45000 reads from 100000 total (45.0%)
Note: 5000 BAM read IDs were not found in POD5 file(s)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Read IDs are matched by UUID from BAM query names
  • Signal data is kept compressed during copying for efficiency
  • Run info is preserved and deduplicated when processing multiple files
  • Reads not found in the BAM file are excluded from output

docs.title: podfive filter
docs.text: Extract specific reads from a POD5 file based on read IDs.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: podfive filter -i <IDS_FILE> -o <OUTPUT> <INPUT>

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description <INPUT> Input POD5 file to filter

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description -i, --ids <FILE> File containing read IDs to extract (required) -o, --output <FILE> Output file path (required) -h, --help Print help

docs.title: ID File Format
docs.text:
The IDs file should contain one UUID per line:

a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
b2c3d4e5-f6a7-8901-bcde-f12345678901
c3d4e5f6-a7b8-9012-cdef-123456789012

docs.title: Supported Formats
docs.text:
• Standard UUID: a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
  • No dashes: a1b2c3d4e5f67890abcdef1234567890
  • Comments (lines starting with #) are ignored
  • Empty lines are ignored

docs.title: Basic Filtering
docs.text:
Create a file with read IDs:

cat > interesting_reads.txt << EOF
a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
b2c3d4e5-f6a7-8901-bcde-f12345678901
EOF

Filter the POD5 file:

podfive filter -i interesting_reads.txt -o filtered.pod5 experiment.pod5

docs.title: Filter from Basecalling Results
docs.text:
If you have basecalling results with read IDs of interest:

# Extract read IDs from a BAM file (requires samtools)
samtools view aligned.bam | cut -f1 | sort -u > mapped_reads.txt

# Filter POD5 to only mapped reads
podfive filter -i mapped_reads.txt -o mapped.pod5 experiment.pod5

docs.title: Filter Using Another POD5 File
docs.text:
Extract reads that exist in another file:

podfive inspect reads reference.pod5 > reference_ids.txt
podfive filter -i reference_ids.txt -o matching.pod5 experiment.pod5

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
The command prints filtering statistics:

Filtering experiment.pod5 using IDs from interesting_reads.txt
Output: filtered.pod5
Loaded 100 read IDs to filter
Filtered 95 reads from 10000 total (0.95%)
Warning: 5 requested IDs were not found in the input file

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Only reads with matching IDs are included in output
  • Run info is preserved for all matching reads
  • Signal data is re-compressed in the output
  • A warning is shown if some requested IDs are not found

docs.title: podfive inspect
docs.text: Inspect POD5 file metadata and contents.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: podfive inspect <SUBCOMMAND> <INPUT>

docs.title: Subcommands
docs.text: Subcommand Description summary Show file summary statistics reads List all read IDs read Show details for a specific read

docs.title: podfive inspect summary
docs.text: Display summary information about a POD5 file.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: podfive inspect summary <INPUT>

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
podfive inspect summary experiment.pod5

Output: 

File: experiment.pod5
Size: 1.2 GB
Reads: 10,543
Batches: 11
Run info entries: 1

Run: abc123-def456
  Flow cell: FAK12345
  Protocol: sequencing_MIN106_DNA
  Sample rate: 4000 Hz
  Started: 2024-01-15 10:30:00

docs.title: podfive inspect reads
docs.text: List all read IDs in the file.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: podfive inspect reads <INPUT>

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
podfive inspect reads experiment.pod5

Output: 

a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
b2c3d4e5-f6a7-8901-bcde-f12345678901
c3d4e5f6-a7b8-9012-cdef-123456789012
...

This is useful for creating filter lists:

podfive inspect reads experiment.pod5 > all_reads.txt

docs.title: podfive inspect read
docs.text: Show detailed information about a specific read.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: podfive inspect read <INPUT> <READ_ID>

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description <INPUT> Path to the POD5 file <READ_ID> UUID of the read to inspect

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
podfive inspect read experiment.pod5 a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890

Output: 

Read: a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
  Channel: 1
  Well: 1
  Read number: 42
  Start sample: 1234567
  Num samples: 50000
  Median before: 210.5 pA
  End reason: signal_positive
  Calibration:
    Offset: -240.5
    Scale: 0.145
  Run info: abc123-def456

docs.title: podfive merge
docs.text: Merge multiple POD5 files into a single output file.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: podfive merge -o <OUTPUT> <INPUT>...

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description <INPUT>... One or more POD5 files to merge

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description -o, --output <FILE> Output file path (required) -h, --help Print help

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The merge command combines multiple POD5 files into a single file. This is useful for:

  • Combining files from different sequencing runs
  • Consolidating files split across multiple output files
  • Creating a single file for downstream analysis

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
• All reads from input files are copied to the output
  • Run info entries are deduplicated by acquisition ID
  • Signal data is re-compressed in the output file
  • The output file uses default compression settings

docs.title: Merge Two Files
docs.text: podfive merge -o combined.pod5 run1.pod5 run2.pod5

docs.title: Merge All Files in Directory
docs.text: podfive merge -o all_data.pod5 *.pod5

docs.title: Merge with Explicit File List
docs.text:
podfive merge -o output.pod5 \
    /data/run1/file1.pod5 \
    /data/run1/file2.pod5 \
    /data/run2/file1.pod5

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
The command prints progress information:

Merging 3 files into combined.pod5
  Processing: run1.pod5
    Added 5000 reads
  Processing: run2.pod5
    Added 4500 reads
  Processing: run3.pod5
    Added 5200 reads
Successfully merged 14700 reads into combined.pod5

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Large files may take significant time to merge
  • Ensure sufficient disk space for the output file
  • The output file will be approximately the sum of input file sizes

docs.title: podfive repack
docs.text: Repack POD5 files to optimize storage and apply current compression settings.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: podfive repack -o <OUTPUT_DIR> [OPTIONS] <FILES>...

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description <FILES>... Input POD5 files to repack

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description -o, --output-dir <DIR> Output directory (required) -f, --force Overwrite existing files -h, --help Print help

docs.title: Repack a Single File
docs.text: podfive repack input.pod5 -o repacked/

docs.title: Repack Multiple Files
docs.text: podfive repack *.pod5 -o repacked/

docs.title: Overwrite Existing Files
docs.text: podfive repack *.pod5 -o repacked/ --force

docs.title: In-Place Repacking
docs.text:
You can safely repack files in place (output to the same directory as input). The command uses temporary files to prevent data corruption:

podfive repack data/*.pod5 -o data/ --force

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
The command prints progress and summary:

Repacking 5 file(s) to repacked/
Repacking [████████████████████████████████████████] 5/5
Repacked 50000 reads across 5 file(s)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Output files retain the same names as input files
  • Signal data is decompressed and re-compressed during repacking
  • Run info and all metadata is preserved
  • Safe for in-place repacking (uses temporary files)

docs.title: podfive subset
docs.text: Split reads from a POD5 file into multiple output files based on a CSV mapping.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: podfive subset --csv <FILE> -o <OUTPUT_DIR> [OPTIONS] <INPUT>

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description <INPUT> Input POD5 file

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description --csv <FILE> CSV file with read_id,output columns (required) -o, --output-dir <DIR> Output directory (default: current directory) -f, --force Overwrite existing files -h, --help Print help

docs.title: CSV File Format
docs.text:
The CSV file must have a header row with read_id and output columns:

read_id,output
a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890,sample1.pod5
b2c3d4e5-f6a7-8901-bcde-f12345678901,sample1.pod5
c3d4e5f6-a7b8-9012-cdef-123456789012,sample2.pod5

docs.title: Supported UUID Formats
docs.text:
• Standard format: a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
  • No dashes: a1b2c3d4e5f67890abcdef1234567890
  • Uppercase: A1B2C3D4-E5F6-7890-ABCD-EF1234567890

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Whitespace is trimmed from fields
  • Empty lines are ignored
  • Each unique output value creates a separate POD5 file

docs.title: Basic Subsetting
docs.text:
Create a mapping file:

cat > mapping.csv << EOF
read_id,output
a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890,barcode01.pod5
b2c3d4e5-f6a7-8901-bcde-f12345678901,barcode01.pod5
c3d4e5f6-a7b8-9012-cdef-123456789012,barcode02.pod5
EOF

Split the POD5 file:

podfive subset experiment.pod5 --csv mapping.csv -o demultiplexed/

docs.title: Demultiplexing by Barcode
docs.text:
If you have demultiplexing results from basecalling:

# Assuming you have a CSV from your demultiplexing pipeline
podfive subset multiplexed.pod5 --csv barcode_assignments.csv -o demux/

docs.title: Splitting by Sample
docs.text:
Split reads into per-sample files:

podfive subset pooled.pod5 --csv sample_mapping.csv -o samples/

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
The command prints a summary of the subsetting operation:

Subsetting 10000 reads into 3 output file(s)
Subsetting [████████████████████████████████████████] 10000/10000

Subset summary:
  Matched reads: 9500
  Unmatched reads: 500

Output files:
  demux/barcode01.pod5 (4000 reads)
  demux/barcode02.pod5 (3500 reads)
  demux/barcode03.pod5 (2000 reads)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Reads not in the CSV mapping are excluded from output
  • Run info is copied to all output files
  • Each output file is a complete, valid POD5 file
  • Signal data is decompressed and re-compressed for each output

docs.title: podfive summary
docs.text: Generate a comprehensive summary of POD5 file(s) including read statistics, QC metrics, and run metadata.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: podfive summary <INPUT> [--json]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description <INPUT> Input POD5 file or directory containing POD5 files

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description --json Output as JSON instead of formatted table -h, --help Print help

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Directory Support: Point to a directory and automatically process all *.pod5 files recursively
  • Read Statistics: N50, mean, median read lengths with distribution sparkline
  • Run Metadata: Flow cell, sequencing kit, sample ID, protocol info
  • QC Metrics: Active channel count, well distribution, end reason breakdown
  • Warnings: Alerts for old POD5 versions or corrupted files

docs.title: Single File
docs.text: podfive summary experiment.pod5

docs.title: Directory of Files
docs.text: podfive summary pod5_pass/

docs.title: JSON Output
docs.text: podfive summary experiment.pod5 --json

docs.title: JSON Output
docs.text:
{
  "files": [
    {
      "path": "experiment.pod5",

      "read_count": 10543,

    }
  ],
  "run_info": {
    "acquisition_id": "abc123-def456",
    "acquisition_start_time": "2024-01-15 10:30 UTC",
    "sample_rate": 4000,
    "flow_cell_id": "FAK12345",
    "flow_cell_product_code": "FLO-MIN106",
    "sequencing_kit": "SQK-LSK109",
    "sample_id": "sample_001",
    "experiment_name": "my_experiment",
    "protocol_name": "sequencing_MIN106_DNA",
    "software": "MinKNOW 23.04.5",
    "system_name": "MinION",
    "system_type": "Mk1C"
  },
  "statistics": {
    "total_samples": 527150000,
    "length_min": 1000,
    "length_max": 500000,
    "length_mean": 50000.0,
    "length_median": 42000,
    "length_n50": 65000,
    "active_channels": 450,
    "total_channels": 512
  },
  "end_reasons": {
    "signal_positive": 8500,
    "signal_negative": 1500,
    "mux_change": 400,
    "unblock_mux_change": 143
  },
  "warnings": []
}

docs.title: Warnings
docs.text:
The command displays warnings for:

  • Old POD5 versions: Files using an older POD5 format version
  • Corrupted files: Files that cannot be opened or read
  • Read errors: Individual reads that fail to parse

Example with warnings:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ POD5 Summary: pod5_pass/ (24 files)                                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
...
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ⚠ WARNINGS                                                                  │
│   2 file(s) could not be read                                               │
│   Corrupted/unreadable file: pod5_pass/bad_file.pod5 (Invalid signature)    │
│   Old POD5 version: legacy.pod5 uses v0.3 (current: 1.0)                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• When processing a directory, all *.pod5 files are found recursively
  • Statistics are aggregated across all files
  • Run info is taken from the first valid file
  • Corrupted files are skipped with a warning, not fatal errors

docs.title: podfive view
docs.text: Display reads from a POD5 file as a formatted table.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: podfive view [OPTIONS] <INPUT>

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description <INPUT> Path to the POD5 file

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description --include <FIELDS> Comma-separated list of fields to include --exclude <FIELDS> Comma-separated list of fields to exclude --ids Only show read IDs -o, --output <FILE> Write output to file instead of stdout -h, --help Print help

docs.title: Output Fields
docs.text:
The default output includes:

Field Description read_id Unique identifier (UUID) channel Channel number well Well number (1-4) read_number Sequential read number start Start sample position num_samples Total signal samples median_before Median current before read end_reason Why the read ended

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
podfive view experiment.pod5

Output: 

read_id                               channel  well  read_number  num_samples
a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890  1        1     1            50000
b2c3d4e5-f6a7-8901-bcde-f12345678901  1        1     2            75000
c3d4e5f6-a7b8-9012-cdef-123456789012  2        1     1            62000
...

docs.title: Show Only Read IDs
docs.text:
podfive view --ids experiment.pod5

Output: 

a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
b2c3d4e5-f6a7-8901-bcde-f12345678901
c3d4e5f6-a7b8-9012-cdef-123456789012

docs.title: Save to File
docs.text: podfive view experiment.pod5 -o reads.tsv

docs.title: Select Specific Fields
docs.text: podfive view --include read_id,channel,num_samples experiment.pod5

docs.title: POD5 Format Overview
docs.text: POD5 is the successor to FAST5 for storing Oxford Nanopore sequencing data. It uses Apache Arrow IPC (Feather V2) for efficient columnar storage with custom signal compression.

docs.title: Design Goals
docs.text:
• Efficient access - Columnar format enables reading specific fields without loading entire reads
  • Compact storage - VBZ compression achieves 60-80% reduction in signal data size
  • Batch processing - Data organized in batches for parallel processing
  • Self-describing - Schema embedded in file, extensible metadata

docs.title: File Structure
docs.text:
A POD5 file contains:

  1. Signature - Magic bytes identifying the file format
  2. Signal Table - Compressed raw signal data (Arrow IPC)
  3. Run Info Table - Sequencing run metadata (Arrow IPC)
  4. Reads Table - Read records with references to signal (Arrow IPC)
  5. Footer - FlatBuffer with table locations and metadata

See Container Structure for detailed layout.

docs.title: Reads Table
docs.text:
One row per read containing:

Field Type Description read_id UUID Unique identifier channel u16 Channel number well u8 Well (1-4) read_number u32 Sequential number start_sample u64 Start position num_samples u64 Signal length median_before f32 Pre-read current calibration_offset f32 ADC offset calibration_scale f32 ADC scale end_reason enum Why read ended signal List Signal row indices run_info u32 Run info index

docs.title: Signal Table
docs.text:
Compressed signal chunks:

Field Type Description signal VBZ binary Compressed signal data samples u32 Number of samples

docs.title: Run Info Table
docs.text:
One row per unique acquisition:

Field Type Description acquisition_id string Unique run ID acquisition_start_time i64 Start timestamp (ms) sample_rate u16 Sampling rate (Hz) adc_min i16 ADC minimum adc_max i16 ADC maximum context_tags Map Key-value metadata tracking_id Map Tracking metadata

docs.title: Signal Compression
docs.text:
Signal data uses the VBZ codec:

  1. Delta encoding - Store differences between samples
  2. Zigzag encoding - Map signed to unsigned integers
  3. SVB16 - Variable-length encoding (1-2 bytes per value)
  4. ZSTD - Final compression

See Compression for algorithm details.

docs.title: Arrow Extension Types
docs.text:
POD5 uses custom Arrow extension types:

Type Base Type Description minknow.uuid FixedSizeBinary(16) UUID storage minknow.vbz LargeBinary VBZ compressed data

docs.title: Version History
docs.text: Version Changes 0 Initial format 1 Added open_pore_level field 2 Modified signal chunking 3 Added predicted_scaling fields 4 Current version

docs.title: Comparison with FAST5
docs.text: Aspect FAST5 POD5 Container HDF5 Arrow IPC Compression gzip/VBZ VBZ only Access pattern Row-oriented Columnar Metadata HDF5 attributes FlatBuffer File size Baseline ~30% smaller Read speed Slower 2-10x faster

docs.title: Signal Compression (VBZ)
docs.text: POD5 uses the VBZ codec for signal compression, combining delta encoding, zigzag encoding, StreamVByte (SVB16), and ZSTD.

docs.title: Compression Pipeline
docs.text:
Raw Signal (i16)
      │
      ▼
┌─────────────┐
│   Delta     │  Δ[i] = signal[i] - signal[i-1]
│  Encoding   │
└─────────────┘
      │
      ▼
┌─────────────┐
│   Zigzag    │  Map signed → unsigned
│  Encoding   │
└─────────────┘
      │
      ▼
┌─────────────┐
│   SVB16     │  Variable-length (1-2 bytes)
│  Encoding   │
└─────────────┘
      │
      ▼
┌─────────────┐
│    ZSTD     │  Final compression
│  Level 1    │
└─────────────┘
      │
      ▼
Compressed Data

docs.title: Delta Encoding
docs.text:
Consecutive signal samples are highly correlated. Delta encoding stores differences:

Original:  [100, 102, 105, 103, 101]
Delta:     [100,   2,   3,  -2,  -2]

Benefits: - Differences are typically small - Smaller values compress better - Preserves signal fidelity exactly

docs.title: Zigzag Encoding
docs.text:
Maps signed integers to unsigned for efficient variable-length encoding:

fn zigzag_encode(val: i16) -> u16 {
    ((val << 1) ^ (val >> 15)) as u16
}

fn zigzag_decode(val: u16) -> i16 {
    ((val >> 1) as i16) ^ (-((val & 1) as i16))
}

Mapping examples: 

 0 →  0
-1 →  1
 1 →  2
-2 →  3
 2 →  4
...

This keeps small magnitudes (positive or negative) as small unsigned values.

docs.title: SVB16 (StreamVByte for 16-bit)
docs.text: Variable-length encoding where each value uses 1 or 2 bytes:

docs.title: Keys Bitmap
docs.text:
• 1 bit per sample, packed 8 per byte
  • Bit = 0: value fits in 1 byte (0-255)
  • Bit = 1: value needs 2 bytes (256-65535)
Keys length = (sample_count + 7) / 8 bytes

docs.title: Data Section
docs.text: Values stored sequentially: - 1-byte values: stored as-is - 2-byte values: stored as little-endian u16

docs.title: Encoding Algorithm
docs.text:
fn svb16_encode(values: &[u16]) -> Vec<u8> {
    let key_len = (values.len() + 7) / 8;
    let mut keys = vec![0u8; key_len];
    let mut data = Vec::new();

    for (i, &val) in values.iter().enumerate() {
        if val > 255 {
            keys[i / 8] |= 1 << (i % 8);
            data.push(val as u8);
            data.push((val >> 8) as u8);
        } else {
            data.push(val as u8);
        }
    }

    let mut result = keys;
    result.extend(data);
    result
}

docs.title: Decoding Algorithm
docs.text:
fn svb16_decode(data: &[u8], count: usize) -> Vec<u16> {
    let key_len = (count + 7) / 8;
    let keys = &data[..key_len];
    let mut data_pos = key_len;
    let mut result = Vec::with_capacity(count);

    for i in 0..count {
        let is_two_byte = (keys[i / 8] >> (i % 8)) & 1 == 1;
        if is_two_byte {
            let val = u16::from_le_bytes([data[data_pos], data[data_pos + 1]]);
            result.push(val);
            data_pos += 2;
        } else {
            result.push(data[data_pos] as u16);
            data_pos += 1;
        }
    }

    result
}

docs.title: ZSTD Compression
docs.text:
Final pass uses ZSTD at level 1:

  • Fast compression/decompression
  • Good compression ratio
  • Streaming support
  • Standard library (libzstd)

Level 1 chosen for balance: - Level 0: No compression - Level 1: Fast, ~60% ratio - Level 3: Default, ~65% ratio - Level 19+: Slow, ~70% ratio

For POD5, level 1 is sufficient as SVB16 already reduces entropy.

docs.title: Compression Ratios
docs.text:
Typical compression ratios (compressed/original):

Stage Ratio Cumulative Delta ~1.0x ~1.0x Zigzag ~1.0x ~1.0x SVB16 ~0.6x ~0.6x ZSTD ~0.5x ~0.3x

Final result: 30-40% of original size

docs.title: Decompression
docs.text:
Reverse the pipeline:

pub fn decompress_signal(data: &[u8], sample_count: usize) -> Result<Vec<i16>> {
    // 1. ZSTD decompress
    let svb_data = zstd::decode_all(data)?;

    // 2. SVB16 decode
    let zigzag_values = svb16_decode(&svb_data, sample_count);

    // 3. Zigzag decode
    let deltas: Vec<i16> = zigzag_values.iter()
        .map(|&v| zigzag_decode(v))
        .collect();

    // 4. Delta decode (cumulative sum)
    let mut signal = Vec::with_capacity(sample_count);
    let mut acc: i16 = 0;
    for delta in deltas {
        acc = acc.wrapping_add(delta);
        signal.push(acc);
    }

    Ok(signal)
}

docs.title: Extension Type
docs.text:
VBZ data uses the Arrow extension type minknow.vbz:

{
    "extension_name": "minknow.vbz",
    "storage_type": "LargeBinary",
    "metadata": {
        "codec": "vbz",
        "version": 1
    }
}

docs.title: Container Structure
docs.text: POD5 files use a custom container format with Arrow IPC tables and a FlatBuffer footer.

docs.title: Signature
docs.text:
The file starts and ends with an 8-byte signature:

Hex:    8B 50 4F 44 0D 0A 1A 0A
ASCII:  \213 P  O  D  
 
 \032

This signature: - Starts with a non-ASCII byte (0x8B) to detect binary/text confusion - Contains POD for identification - Uses to detect line-ending corruption - Ends with as final newline

docs.title: Section Markers
docs.text: 16-byte UUID markers separate major sections. These provide: - Clear section boundaries - Corruption detection - Forward compatibility

docs.title: Arrow IPC Tables
docs.text:
Each table is stored as an Arrow IPC stream:

  1. Schema message - Column names and types
  2. RecordBatch messages - Data in columnar format

Tables can contain multiple batches for large files.

docs.title: Footer Structure
docs.text:
The footer is a FlatBuffer containing:

table Footer {
    file_identifier: string;      // "POD5"
    software: string;             // Writer software name
    section_marker: [ubyte];      // 16-byte UUID

    contents: EmbeddedFile;       // File manifest
    reads: EmbeddedFile;          // Reads table location
    run_info: EmbeddedFile;       // Run info location
    signal: EmbeddedFile;         // Signal location
}

table EmbeddedFile {
    offset: long;                 // Byte offset from start
    length: long;                 // Length in bytes
}

docs.title: Reading Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Read last 8 bytes to verify signature
  2. Read footer length (8 bytes before final signature)
  3. Parse footer FlatBuffer
  4. Use offsets to locate each table
  5. Parse Arrow IPC streams on demand
// Pseudocode
let file_len = file.metadata().len();
let sig_end = &file[file_len - 8..];
assert!(sig_end == SIGNATURE);

let footer_len = read_i64(&file[file_len - 8 - 16 - 8..]);
let footer_start = file_len - 8 - 16 - 8 - footer_len;
let footer = parse_footer(&file[footer_start..]);

// Access tables via footer offsets
let reads_data = &file[footer.reads.offset..][..footer.reads.length];

docs.title: Memory Mapping
docs.text:
For large files, memory mapping is recommended:

  • File is mapped to virtual memory
  • Only accessed pages are loaded
  • OS handles caching and eviction
  • Multiple processes can share mapped pages

docs.title: Batch Organization
docs.text:
Data is organized in batches for:

  • Parallel processing - Each batch processed independently
  • Memory efficiency - Load one batch at a time
  • Streaming writes - Flush batches incrementally

Typical batch sizes: - Reads: 1,000-10,000 reads per batch - Signal: 100-1,000 chunks per batch

docs.title: Integrity Checks
docs.text:
The format provides several integrity checks:

  1. Signature validation - File starts/ends with magic bytes
  2. Section markers - UUIDs at section boundaries
  3. Footer checksum - Optional CRC in footer
  4. Arrow validation - Schema consistency checks

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Rust 1.70 or later
  • Cargo (comes with Rust)

docs.title: From Source
docs.text:
Clone the repository and build:

git clone https://github.com/rnabioco/podfive-rs.git
cd podfive-rs
cargo build --release

The binary will be at target/release/podfive. You can copy it to a directory in your PATH:

cp target/release/podfive ~/.local/bin/
# or
sudo cp target/release/podfive /usr/local/bin/

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
podfive --version
podfive --help

docs.title: Using the Library
docs.text:
Add podfive-core to your Cargo.toml:

[dependencies]
podfive-core = { git = "https://github.com/rnabioco/podfive-rs.git" }

Or if published to crates.io:

[dependencies]
podfive-core = "0.1"

docs.title: Building Documentation
docs.text:
To build the API documentation locally:

cargo doc --open

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text:
For contributing to podfive-rs:

# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/rnabioco/podfive-rs.git
cd podfive-rs

# Run tests
cargo test

# Run clippy lints
cargo clippy

# Format code
cargo fmt

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide will help you get started with podfive-rs in just a few minutes.

docs.title: Viewing POD5 Files
docs.text:
The simplest way to explore a POD5 file is with the view command:

podfive view experiment.pod5

This displays a table of reads with key information like read ID, channel, and sample count.

docs.title: Inspecting File Details
docs.text:
Get a summary of the file:

podfive inspect summary experiment.pod5

Output: 

File: experiment.pod5
Reads: 10,000
Run info entries: 1
File size: 1.2 GB

List all reads:

podfive inspect reads experiment.pod5

docs.title: Merging Files
docs.text:
Combine multiple POD5 files from a sequencing run:

podfive merge -o combined.pod5 file1.pod5 file2.pod5 file3.pod5

docs.title: Filtering Reads
docs.text:
Create a file with read IDs you want to extract (one UUID per line):

a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
b2c3d4e5-f6a7-8901-bcde-f12345678901

Then filter:

podfive filter -i read_ids.txt -o filtered.pod5 experiment.pod5

docs.title: Reading a POD5 File
docs.text:
use podfive_core::{Reader, Result};

fn main() -> Result<()> {
    // Open a POD5 file
    let reader = Reader::open("experiment.pod5")?;

    // Print basic info
    println!("Total reads: {}", reader.read_count()?);

    // Iterate over reads
    for read_result in reader.reads()? {
        let read = read_result?;

        println!("Read: {}", read.read_id);
        println!("  Channel: {}", read.channel);
        println!("  Samples: {}", read.num_samples);

        // Get the raw signal data
        let signal = reader.get_signal(&read.signal_rows)?;

        // Calculate mean signal
        let mean: f64 = signal.iter()
            .map(|&s| s as f64)
            .sum::<f64>() / signal.len() as f64;
        println!("  Mean signal: {:.2}", mean);
    }

    Ok(())
}

docs.title: Accessing Run Information
docs.text:
use podfive_core::Reader;

let reader = Reader::open("experiment.pod5")?;

for run_info in reader.run_infos() {
    println!("Acquisition ID: {}", run_info.acquisition_id);
    println!("Sample rate: {} Hz", run_info.sample_rate);
    println!("Flow cell: {}", run_info.flow_cell_id);
    println!("Protocol: {}", run_info.protocol_name);

    // Context tags contain experiment metadata
    for (key, value) in &run_info.context_tags {
        println!("  {}: {}", key, value);
    }
}

docs.title: Writing a POD5 File
docs.text:
use podfive_core::{Writer, WriterOptions, ReadData, RunInfoData};

fn main() -> podfive_core::Result<()> {
    // Create a new POD5 file
    let mut writer = Writer::create("output.pod5", WriterOptions::default())?;

    // Add run information
    let run_info = RunInfoData {
        acquisition_id: "my_experiment".to_string(),
        sample_rate: 4000,
        flow_cell_id: "FAK12345".to_string(),
        ..Default::default()
    };
    let run_idx = writer.add_run_info(run_info)?;

    // Add reads with signal data
    for i in 0..10 {
        let read = ReadData {
            read_id: uuid::Uuid::new_v4(),
            read_number: i + 1,
            channel: 1,
            run_info_index: run_idx,
            num_samples: 1000,
            ..Default::default()
        };

        // Generate or provide signal data
        let signal: Vec<i16> = (0..1000)
            .map(|j| ((j as f64 * 0.1).sin() * 100.0) as i16)
            .collect();

        writer.add_read(read, &signal)?;
    }

    // Finalize the file
    writer.finish()?;

    Ok(())
}

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• CLI Reference - Full documentation of all commands
  • Library Guide - Detailed library usage
  • File Format - Understanding the POD5 format

docs.title: Library Overview
docs.text: The podfive-core library provides Rust APIs for reading and writing POD5 files.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Read POD5 files - Memory-mapped, efficient reading of reads and signal data
  • Write POD5 files - Create new POD5 files with automatic compression
  • Signal compression - VBZ codec (SVB16 + ZSTD) for signal data
  • Full metadata support - Read/write run info, calibration, and pore data

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
use podfive_core::{Reader, Writer, WriterOptions, ReadData, RunInfoData};

// Read a POD5 file
let reader = Reader::open("input.pod5")?;
println!("File contains {} reads", reader.read_count());

for read in reader.reads() {
    println!("Read {} has {} samples", read.read_id, read.num_samples);
}

// Write a new POD5 file
let mut writer = Writer::create("output.pod5", WriterOptions::default())?;
writer.add_run_info(run_info)?;
writer.add_read(read_data, &signal)?;
writer.finish()?;

docs.title: Modules
docs.text: Module Description Reading Reading POD5 files Writing Creating POD5 files Types Data structures and types

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
All operations return Result<T, podfive_core::Error>. The error type provides detailed information about what went wrong:

use podfive_core::{Reader, Error};

match Reader::open("file.pod5") {
    Ok(reader) => { /* use reader */ }
    Err(Error::Io(e)) => eprintln!("I/O error: {}", e),
    Err(Error::InvalidSignature) => eprintln!("Not a valid POD5 file"),
    Err(e) => eprintln!("Error: {}", e),
}

docs.title: Reading POD5 Files
docs.text: The Reader struct provides efficient access to POD5 file contents.

docs.title: Opening a File
docs.text:
use podfive_core::Reader;

let reader = Reader::open("experiment.pod5")?;

The file is memory-mapped for efficient access. The reader validates the file signature and parses the footer on open.

docs.title: File Information
docs.text:
// Number of reads in the file
let count = reader.read_count();

// Number of batches (internal structure)
let batches = reader.batch_count();

// Access run info
for (i, run_info) in reader.run_infos().iter().enumerate() {
    println!("Run {}: {}", i, run_info.acquisition_id);
}

docs.title: Iterating Over Reads
docs.text:
// Iterate over all reads
for read in reader.reads() {
    println!("Read: {}", read.read_id);
    println!("  Channel: {}", read.channel);
    println!("  Samples: {}", read.num_samples);
    println!("  End reason: {:?}", read.end_reason);
}

docs.title: Accessing Signal Data
docs.text:
Signal data is stored separately and must be explicitly requested:

// Get signal for a specific read
let signal: Vec<i16> = reader.get_signal(&read)?;

// Signal is raw ADC values
println!("Signal length: {}", signal.len());
println!("First 10 samples: {:?}", &signal[..10.min(signal.len())]);

docs.title: Converting to Physical Units
docs.text:
The signal is stored as raw ADC values. Convert to picoamps (pA) using calibration:

fn to_picoamps(signal: &[i16], offset: f32, scale: f32) -> Vec<f32> {
    signal.iter()
        .map(|&s| (s as f32 + offset) * scale)
        .collect()
}

let pa_signal = to_picoamps(&signal, read.calibration_offset, read.calibration_scale);

docs.title: Finding Specific Reads
docs.text:
Find reads by their UUIDs:

use uuid::Uuid;

let target_ids = vec![
    Uuid::parse_str("a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890")?,
];

let found_reads = reader.find_reads(&target_ids)?;

docs.title: Batch Access
docs.text:
For advanced use cases, access raw Arrow batches:

for batch_idx in 0..reader.batch_count() {
    let batch = reader.read_batch(batch_idx)?;
    println!("Batch {} has {} rows", batch_idx, batch.num_rows());
}

docs.title: Run Info Access
docs.text:
Access run information by index:

// Get run info for a read
let run_info = reader.get_run_info(read.run_info_index)?;

println!("Acquisition ID: {}", run_info.acquisition_id);
println!("Sample rate: {} Hz", run_info.sample_rate);
println!("Flow cell: {:?}", run_info.tracking_id.get("flow_cell_id"));

docs.title: Memory Efficiency
docs.text:
The reader uses memory-mapping, so:

  • Only accessed data is loaded into memory
  • Large files don't consume proportional RAM
  • Multiple readers can share the same memory-mapped data
// Safe to open very large files
let reader = Reader::open("large_file.pod5")?;  // Doesn't load entire file

docs.title: Types Reference
docs.text: Core data structures used throughout the library.

docs.title: ReadData
docs.text:
Represents a single nanopore read.

pub struct ReadData {
    /// Unique identifier for the read
    pub read_id: Uuid,

    /// Sequential read number within the channel
    pub read_number: u32,

    /// Sample position where read started
    pub start_sample: u64,

    /// Channel number (1-512 typically)
    pub channel: u16,

    /// Well number (1-4)
    pub well: u8,

    /// Pore type string
    pub pore_type: String,

    /// Calibration offset for converting ADC to pA
    pub calibration_offset: f32,

    /// Calibration scale for converting ADC to pA
    pub calibration_scale: f32,

    /// Median current level before read started
    pub median_before: f32,

    /// Why the read ended
    pub end_reason: EndReason,

    /// Whether end reason was forced by software
    pub end_reason_forced: bool,

    /// Index into run info table
    pub run_info_index: u32,

    /// Number of MinKNOW events
    pub num_minknow_events: u64,

    /// Total number of signal samples
    pub num_samples: u64,

    /// Signal row indices (internal use)
    pub signal_rows: Vec<u64>,
}

docs.title: Converting Signal to Physical Units
docs.text:
fn adc_to_picoamps(adc: i16, read: &ReadData) -> f32 {
    (adc as f32 + read.calibration_offset) * read.calibration_scale
}

docs.title: RunInfoData
docs.text:
Metadata about a sequencing run.

pub struct RunInfoData {
    /// Unique acquisition identifier
    pub acquisition_id: String,

    /// Start time in milliseconds since epoch
    pub acquisition_start_time_ms: i64,

    /// Maximum ADC value
    pub adc_max: i16,

    /// Minimum ADC value
    pub adc_min: i16,

    /// Sampling rate in Hz
    pub sample_rate: u16,

    /// Context tags (key-value metadata)
    pub context_tags: HashMap<String, String>,

    /// Tracking ID metadata
    pub tracking_id: HashMap<String, String>,
}

docs.title: Common Tracking ID Fields
docs.text: Key Description flow_cell_id Flow cell identifier device_id Sequencer device ID sample_id User-provided sample name experiment_id Experiment identifier protocol_group_id Protocol group

docs.title: EndReason
docs.text:
Why a read ended.

pub enum EndReason {
    Unknown,
    MuxChange,
    UnblockMuxChange,
    DataServiceUnblockMuxChange,
    SignalPositive,
    SignalNegative,
}
Variant Description Unknown Reason not recorded MuxChange Mux changed to different well UnblockMuxChange Unblock triggered mux change DataServiceUnblockMuxChange Data service triggered unblock SignalPositive Normal end, positive signal SignalNegative Normal end, negative signal

docs.title: Error
docs.text:
Error types returned by library operations.

pub enum Error {
    /// I/O error
    Io(std::io::Error),

    /// Invalid POD5 file signature
    InvalidSignature,

    /// Invalid footer structure
    InvalidFooter(String),

    /// Arrow error during IPC operations
    Arrow(arrow::error::ArrowError),

    /// Compression/decompression error
    Compression(String),

    /// Missing required field
    MissingField(String),

    /// Read not found
    ReadNotFound(Uuid),
}

docs.title: Error Handling Example
docs.text:
use podfive_core::{Reader, Error};

fn process_file(path: &str) -> Result<(), String> {
    let reader = Reader::open(path).map_err(|e| match e {
        Error::Io(io_err) => format!("Cannot open file: {}", io_err),
        Error::InvalidSignature => "Not a valid POD5 file".to_string(),
        Error::InvalidFooter(msg) => format!("Corrupt file: {}", msg),
        _ => format!("Error: {}", e),
    })?;

    // Process file...
    Ok(())
}

docs.title: WriterOptions
docs.text:
Configuration for file writing.

pub struct WriterOptions {
    /// Enable VBZ signal compression (default: true)
    pub signal_compression: bool,

    /// Maximum samples per signal chunk (default: 102400)
    pub signal_chunk_size: u32,
}

docs.title: UUID Handling
docs.text:
Read IDs are UUIDs stored as 16-byte fixed-size binary:

use uuid::Uuid;

// Parse from string
let id = Uuid::parse_str("a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890")?;

// Generate new
let new_id = Uuid::new_v4();

// Access bytes
let bytes: &[u8; 16] = id.as_bytes();

docs.title: Writing POD5 Files
docs.text: The Writer struct creates new POD5 files with automatic signal compression.

docs.title: Creating a Writer
docs.text:
use podfive_core::{Writer, WriterOptions};

// Default options
let writer = Writer::create("output.pod5", WriterOptions::default())?;

// Custom options
let options = WriterOptions {
    signal_compression: true,
    signal_chunk_size: 102_400,
    ..Default::default()
};
let writer = Writer::create("output.pod5", options)?;

docs.title: Adding Run Info
docs.text:
Every read references a run info entry. Add run info before adding reads:

use podfive_core::RunInfoData;
use std::collections::HashMap;

let run_info = RunInfoData {
    acquisition_id: "abc123-def456".to_string(),
    acquisition_start_time_ms: 1705320600000,
    adc_max: 2047,
    adc_min: -2048,
    sample_rate: 4000,
    context_tags: HashMap::new(),
    tracking_id: {
        let mut m = HashMap::new();
        m.insert("flow_cell_id".to_string(), "FAK12345".to_string());
        m
    },
    ..Default::default()
};

let run_info_index = writer.add_run_info(run_info)?;

docs.title: Adding Reads
docs.text:
Add reads with their signal data:

use podfive_core::{ReadData, EndReason};
use uuid::Uuid;

let read = ReadData {
    read_id: Uuid::new_v4(),
    read_number: 1,
    start_sample: 0,
    channel: 1,
    well: 1,
    pore_type: "not_set".to_string(),
    calibration_offset: -240.0,
    calibration_scale: 0.145,
    median_before: 210.5,
    end_reason: EndReason::SignalPositive,
    end_reason_forced: false,
    run_info_index,
    num_minknow_events: 100,
    num_samples: 50000,
    ..Default::default()
};

// Signal as raw ADC values
let signal: Vec<i16> = vec![/* ... */];

writer.add_read(read, &signal)?;

docs.title: Finishing the File
docs.text:
Always call finish() to write the footer and finalize the file:

writer.finish()?;

The file is not valid until finish() completes successfully.

docs.title: Complete Example
docs.text:
use podfive_core::{Writer, WriterOptions, ReadData, RunInfoData, EndReason};
use uuid::Uuid;
use std::collections::HashMap;

fn write_pod5_file() -> Result<(), podfive_core::Error> {
    let mut writer = Writer::create("output.pod5", WriterOptions::default())?;

    // Add run info
    let run_info = RunInfoData {
        acquisition_id: "run-001".to_string(),
        sample_rate: 4000,
        adc_max: 2047,
        adc_min: -2048,
        ..Default::default()
    };
    let run_idx = writer.add_run_info(run_info)?;

    // Add some reads
    for i in 0..10 {
        let read = ReadData {
            read_id: Uuid::new_v4(),
            read_number: i + 1,
            channel: ((i % 512) + 1) as u16,
            well: ((i % 4) + 1) as u8,
            run_info_index: run_idx,
            num_samples: 10000,
            end_reason: EndReason::SignalPositive,
            ..Default::default()
        };

        // Generate dummy signal
        let signal: Vec<i16> = (0..10000).map(|j| ((j % 500) as i16) - 250).collect();

        writer.add_read(read, &signal)?;
    }

    writer.finish()?;
    Ok(())
}

docs.title: WriterOptions
docs.text: Option Default Description signal_compression true Enable VBZ compression for signal signal_chunk_size 102400 Max samples per signal chunk

docs.title: Signal Compression
docs.text:
By default, signal data is compressed using the VBZ codec:

  1. Delta encoding - Store differences between consecutive samples
  2. Zigzag encoding - Map signed integers to unsigned
  3. SVB16 - Variable-length encoding (1-2 bytes per sample)
  4. ZSTD - Final compression pass

This typically achieves 60-80% compression on nanopore signal data.

docs.title: Copying Reads Between Files
docs.text:
use podfive_core::{Reader, Writer, WriterOptions};

let reader = Reader::open("input.pod5")?;
let mut writer = Writer::create("output.pod5", WriterOptions::default())?;

// Copy run infos
for run_info in reader.run_infos() {
    writer.add_run_info(run_info.clone())?;
}

// Copy specific reads
for read in reader.reads() {
    if should_include(&read) {
        let signal = reader.get_signal(&read)?;
        writer.add_read(read, &signal)?;
    }
}

writer.finish()?;
